_comment: English → Japanese | Month 1 Week 1 | Lesson — Script & Greetings
title: Introduction to Japanese: Hiragana & Greetings
description: Begin your Japanese journey by learning Hiragana vowels and essential daily greetings.

blocks.title: Welcome to Japanese! 日本語へようこそ！
blocks.body:
Japanese (日本語 — Nihongo) is one of the world's most unique and fascinating languages, spoken by over 128 million people in Japan.

Japanese uses THREE writing systems:
• ひらがな (Hiragana) — for native Japanese words
• カタカナ (Katakana) — for foreign/borrowed words
• 漢字 (Kanji) — characters borrowed from Chinese

We'll start with Hiragana — the foundation of Japanese writing!

🎯 Good news: Japanese pronunciation is very regular and phonetic — what you see is what you say!

blocks.title: What You'll Learn This Week
points: The 5 Hiragana vowels: あ, い, う, え, お (a, i, u, e, o)
Essential greetings: Hello, Thank you, Sorry, Goodbye
Polite form (丁寧語 — Teineigo) — always use this with strangers

blocks.title: The 5 Hiragana Vowels (母音 — Boin)
blocks.body:
Japanese has 5 pure vowels — and they NEVER change:

あ (A) — like 'a' in 'father' — Example: あお (ao) = blue
い (I) — like 'ee' in 'see' — Example: いぬ (inu) = dog
う (U) — like 'oo' but lips not rounded — Example: うみ (umi) = sea
え (E) — like 'e' in 'bed' — Example: えき (eki) = train station
お (O) — like 'o' in 'go' — Example: おかあさん (okaasan) = mother

💡 Key insight: Japanese vowels sound similar to Spanish vowels! Each syllable is pronounced clearly and evenly.

blocks.title: Essential Greetings — At a Glance
rows: ありがとうございます

lines.text: こんにちは！おなまえは？
わたしの なまえは ___です。
はじめまして！よろしくおねがいします。
lines.translation: Nice to meet you! Please treat me kindly.

lines.text: こちらこそ、よろしくおねがいします！

blocks.title: Japanese Sentence Structure: SOV Order

examples.target: わたしは にほんごを べんきょうします。
examples.translation: Watashi wa Nihongo wo benkyou shimasu.

examples.target: かれは みずを のみます。

blocks.note: Unlike English (SVO), Japanese puts the verb at the END of the sentence! Think: 'I Japanese study' instead of 'I study Japanese.' です (desu) means 'is/am/are'.

blocks.body: 💡 Important tip: The particle は (ha) is read as 'WA' when used as a topic marker! So わたし**は** = watashi **wa** (I/me). This is one of Japanese's most common 'tricky' rules.

blocks.title: Fill in the Blanks
blocks.instructions: Write the correct Japanese word or phrase:

items.answer: ありがとうございます

items.sentence: In Japanese sentences, the verb comes ___.

blocks.title: Bowing in Japan — お辞儀 (Ojigi)
blocks.content: In Japan, people bow (お辞儀 — Ojigi) instead of shaking hands for greetings. The degree of bow shows the level of respect: 15° = casual greeting, 30° = respectful greeting, 45° = deep apology. Just like a handshake in English-speaking cultures, the bow is a crucial part of Japanese social etiquette!

blocks.title: Week 1 Lesson — Can you do this?
items: Say 'hello' in three different ways in Japanese (morning/afternoon/evening)
Introduce yourself: 'My name is ___' in Japanese
Say thank you and sorry in Japanese
Recognize the 5 Hiragana vowels (あ,い,う,え,お)
